Okay, so now just to review satellite photo, we are advertising as multiplied salesmanship and two parts of salesmanship, how to open the sale and then how to close the sale. I have a picture up here, it's a famous painting of the Tower of Babel and this is really where advertising is today. As I said before, there was a time in my copyrighted career where it all came together for me with the help of other people. We were running a small ad agency, I'll go into this more later. We wanted to end this problem. I worked my way up to become a copy chief and the business was doing very well to get a little ad agency and I had to hire other people and I had to come up with a way of the copywriters. I had to come up with a way to teach copywriters how to think. I didn't have my thinking as well formulated as I expressed this morning about how to think like the prospect and not like a marketer. But over time, the system that I helped to form there and have refined over these last years has really made that possible. This is a system that I would like to teach you now and in fact, I call it the Bencevenger persuasion equation. In fact, if I were to ask you how many people here would be interested in knowing a very simple four-part formula for knowing how to be in perfect sync with your best prospects and also the same four-point formula would allow anybody in your organization, whether they're a designer or a writer or marketing manager, that they too would be able to make very valuable contributions to the advertising. If you would be interested in that and also how it could be very simple for you with mathematical precision to grade any direct marketing ad and be very close to how it's going to pull. How many would like to know that? Okay. Well, I kind of expected that response. In fact, I'm using this Bencevenger persuasion equation now with you. Once I didn't say on you because all persuasion really begins with the person that you're trying to help or sell something to or serve. It all starts with what the person wants. So many times I've seen marketers go wrong in trying to sell something because they want to sell it, not because anybody wants to buy it.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

And that's again, thinking like a marketer, not like a prospect. It's like the great Dale Carnegie said, when he goes fishing, he loves strawberry short cake, but if he puts that on the end of his line, the fish likes something else. So we always have to be aware of what our prospects want. In fact, probably one of the most persuasive people of the 20th century was Bernard Baruch. Many people here may have heard of him. He was a great financier, Wall Street Titan, and he was an advisor to six presidents. Bernard Baruch had the unique ability, he would be able to walk into any room and you can imagine on Wall Street and down in Washington there were egos that had trouble getting in through the front door. He could talk to a group of people like that and 20 minutes, a half hour later, everyone will come out of the meeting and say, Bernard Baruch has a solution. He's made us all happy with it and he's going to explain it. And he was just brilliant at persuading others to see his point of view. And so at the end of his career, towards the end of his life, somebody said, Mr. Baruch, what is your secret? Is that something that the rest of us can learn how to do? And he gave, he said to that person a 12 word sentence, which I'm going to share with you now, which is the heart of all persuasion, whether it's in print and person or whatever. Those 12 words are 12 words for marketers to live by, find out what people want and show them how to get it. Because that's how he would persuade those big egos in the room. He would say to each sit down with each one, look, your bottom line, what is it you're trying to get out of this deal? And they would say it. Then we go to the next person, then we figure out a way, well, look, there's a way we can make all of these things happen and they'd have a deal. So this is the foundation of all persuasion. And we're going to be getting into this four part formula for great persuasion of thinking, that this doesn't teach you right now how to write great ads or design great ads. That'll come later. The most important point that we want to know now is how to be in perfect sync with what our prospects want. Because too often as marketers thinking as the fishermen, not as the fish, we do what we think will work and we're looking at it totally through our end of the telescope, not through the prospects end. So first and foremost, we want to find out what people want and then show them how to get it. Now you think this would be obvious, but advertising like any field can get caught up in a lot of jargon, psychographics, demographics, all kinds of relationship marketing. I feel that problems are markets. And when I say problems, I mean problems, wants, needs, desires. I'm lumping them all together. Just in the shorthand calling it problems. I know some people in selling and in advertising say, well, you've got to sell what people want, not what they really need. And to me, that's sort of a semantic difference.

## [06:01 - 09:02]

And just again, to keep everything simple, my mom last week has fainted and we took her to the hospital and she needed a pacemaker put in. Thank goodness she's fine now. But I'll tell you, she had that procedure and there were other people in that hospital who were scheduled to have their chest cut open and their arteries worked on, not because they wanted it, but they were lining up to have that done because they needed it. So to me, it's because they want to live. So to make a big distinction between needs and wants, to me, is a semantic difference. So we're going to just call everybody's wants problems. So we want to find what the biggest problems are for the people that we want to serve. I take an aspirin only if I have a headache. You can be the most brilliant copywriter in the world. You're not going to convince me to take an aspirin if I don't have a headache. So that's how you have to think of your prospects. You find those with the greatest need and who really need what you offer. Don't waste your time trying to offer something to people that you have to educate or coax or in any way persuade who don't have a need or want for what you're offering. You'll waste a lot of time, a lot of money doing that. So problems of markets, not demographics. I take an aspirin when I have a headache, not because I want a relationship with my drugist. I don't do anything unless I have a want or a desire. And you would think, well, this is so obvious. It's not really obvious because so many of our lives we spend being the marketer and not looking at it through the prospects' eyes. Classic example is the Edsel. In the 1950s, poor Edsel Ford got one of the greatest failures in marketing history named after him and probably had never lived it down. But the Ford Edsel was something that was a problem actually. The foundation for it was that Ford had a big problem in the middle of its product line. A lot of people, ever since the days of the Model T, that when you buy your entry car, a lot of people would buy a Ford. When you really started doing well in your career, you would buy a Lincoln Continental. But they were losing a lot of sales in between to General Motors who had lots of different models in the middle stages. The bright lights at Ford, including a lot of Harvard MBAs and brilliant marketing people, said we've got to solve this problem. And the Edsel was the answer to this problem. The problem was they spent $350 million in 1950s money. So it's got to be worth at least 10 times that today. So they spent an incredible $3.5 billion launching this product. And then they found nobody wants it. Because the problem was that automobile buyers weren't waking up in the morning saying, my goodness, I am so worried about the middle of Ford products line. I don't know how I'm going to get through this day. So it was a big problem inside the Ford building. Nobody could care less outside the Ford building. So that classic example could show that even the best and brightest can fall into this trap of trying to sell something because they want to sell it, not because people want to buy it.

## [09:02 - 12:10]

Here's the formula. It's a basic form. Starts with what the problem want or desire. The advertiser or salesperson makes a promise to solve that or fulfill that promise, the problem want or desire. And if you touch these four bases that I'm going to walk through now, starts with a real problem, want or need. The advertiser makes a promise to satisfy it. You provide proof that you are solving the problem in a way that's believable. And then base number four is you give a proposition for acting now and you have persuasion. It's as simple as that, truly as simple as that. So now we talked before about how to open the sale. Now we're into the phase of how to close the sale. Applying salesmanship and advertising. We open the sale, making the advertising itself valuable. Now here's the second part of what we do. Now we're into how do you close the sale? You start with what somebody wants. You promise to solve the problem. You prove that you can deliver on what you're promising. You make an irresistible proposition. Sale is done. In fact, we can crank it up just as Emeril would say. Let's kick it up a notch. You'll really get outstanding results if you solve an urgent problem. If your promise is unique, it's not the same promise as many other people are making. We also see weight loss ads. So many of them seem alike. And people will screen out the ones that they've seen a hundred times because it's not unique. So if we really want to crank up this formula to the max, we want to solve an urgent problem that a marketplace is feeling. We want to offer that marketplace a unique promise. We want to offer unquestionable proof. And you can have any adjectives and nouns you want to choose. I just chose the adjective with a U so it sounds a little more easy to remember and the noun with a P. But then we want to offer a user-friendly proposition where they should act now and it's really easy to take up the offer. Now, this is so important because we have to remember that in multiplied salesmanship, you really don't have any power. People say, well, what are the most powerful words and advertise the most powerful things you can do? Again, it's thinking like a marketer. You have no power. If you're talking anonymously to a million people at once, they each one of those people has the total power to toss your ad or ignore it, run to the fridge, whatever they want to do, you have no direct power in that situation except for one. And that is to anticipate what they are likely to think at each point of from the moment of when you introduce that you're trying to solve a problem in their lives. So now we are finally coming full circle to really what is good salesmanship? Again, we open the sale with something of value so that people aren't tossing us away immediately. And now we have the other key components of closing the sale because if we think of salesmanship,

really it's a science of anticipating the prospects objections. So let me just put up a slide here. The five universal objections that every salesperson will encounter, no matter what they're selling in print or in person, well, I have no time. I'm just too busy. Okay, so then the next step is no interest. And if you do manage to convince them that it's interesting, well, you're offering is no difference than what everybody else. So I'm out of here. And so you have to anticipate that objection. Number four objection, this pretty much goes in sequence in a person's mind. Well, okay, you got me to pay attention. But I really didn't think I had an interest. Okay, it is solving a major issue in my life for me or giving me something I'd really like to have or do or be. How your prospects, you know, we're all busy. We're all prospects also. How often do we have to escape from a sales situation? We just don't want to deal with right now. So these are the five major objections or in effect, five major exits of escape that your prospects always will run for. And what you're going to do is you're going to lock the door at each exit. And then the proposition says, no, can't think it over. If you act by a certain date or only a certain number of people are going to get advantage, take advantage of this, you're closing off all the five avenues of escape that every salesperson has to put up with to which they're losing their customers.

## [15:13 - 18:15]

This is so simple that even a child can do it. And then I'm going to show you how. Let's say that you and I have just been, well, in the month or so, have been let off for the summer and we're not going to go to camp. We're good buddies. We'll live next door to each other. Eight years old, nine years old. And we're sitting on your front porch. And say, what are we going to do for the summer? It's, you know, a long summer. What can we do? So, you said, well, it's nice to make some money. So, I lemonade stand. That's so yesterday. And nobody buys lemonade today. They're rolling atkins in this town. What are we going to do? And so, we say, you know, the lake is very popular with fishermen, getting back to this fishing analogy. Why don't we try to sell them worms? We know we're going to dig up the worms every fall. The sanitation department dumps tons of leaves collected from the town into the end of the park. And those leaves become fertilizer for worms. And we can dig up hundreds of worms out there. So, our product is free. So, why don't we dig up some worms? And on Saturday morning, when many of the fishermen come to the lake to launch their boats, we'll sell them boxes of worms. We'll get a little, like, gnolding boxes from the bakery and sell them worms. So, yeah, it's worth a try. We're not doing anything else. So, we do that. And we need an advertising campaign now. Just being kids, we hire Gary Bencevenga to do the art. And this is what they wind up with. And so, we put out a little sign and it says worms. And we sell, you know, a fair number of boxes. We make a few bucks. And we think that we could be doing better. I mean, as it is, this is probably a better ad than half of the Fortune 500 are running on TV. Have you ever noticed that, you know, what were they just trying to sell me or tell me or do what? What was that about? And that's if you're even paying attention to that. So, we ambushed Johnny's dad on the way home from the train, because we know he works on Madison Avenue and we meet him at the train station and we say, Mr. Jones, you know, we're trying to build a little business here for the summer. Here's our ad. Can you make this better? And he said, well, I like your entrepreneurial spirit. Let me take a look. And he said, you know, it's nice that you're saying what the product is, but good advertising should have a benefit. You should think of who your prospects are. And so, we go back with our crayons or hire Gary again. And once again, the artwork is stellar. And we come up with worms that catch fish. And Johnny's dad said, that's the ticket. And so, we go down to the lake again on Saturday morning. And we do see an increase in sales. But we see a lot of people kind of, a lot of fishermen just kind of look at the sign and kind of smile at our business spirit, but they don't buy. And we don't know why. And we did a little better than last week, but we heard that Patricia's mom is a great saleswoman. So, we take our ad campaign to her on Monday.

## [18:15 - 21:19]

And we say, you know, Mr. Smith, can you help us here? Because we thought this was such a great headline. This is where most advertisers who really think they know what they're doing are. Just put a big benefit in a headline for the right market, and that's what good advertising is. So, she tells us, you know, I just got back from this persuasion equation seminar. I have to tell you, that's not enough. She said, you know, you're missing two other steps. You're missing proof. And so, we said, what do you mean proof? He said, well, really, that's just a claim. And people are bombarded by thousands of claims a day, and they just write them off. It's like water off a duck's back. So, that's good in the old days of advertising, but not today. That's not good enough in today's market, where the biggest enemy is clutter, and promises hit us from every direction. So, you have to get some proof. And so, our kids say, well, how do I prove that these worms catch fish? So, where did you get the word? The key to proof is deeper research. And so, she said, look, you know, there's some old-time fishermen down where you kids dig up the leaves. Ask them, why do they find their worms there? They'll give you some, I'm sure they know the lake better than anybody. So, we decide to do that. So, we go down and we visit the old timers, who dig up their worms every Friday. And we said, you know, we're trying to get a little business going. Can you guys tell us why do you dig up your worms here? And they say, and one of the old timers might say to us, oh, look, I admire your entrepreneurial spirit. Let me tell you something. Fish, like most animals, are very wary, like most people. And if something's very unfamiliar to them, they back off. Now, these are the worms that these fish eat all the time. You know, every time it rains, a lot of the worms get washed into the lake, and that's their diet. They're not wary of these worms. This is what's familiar to them. So, these worms catch more, really do catch more fish, and my buddies and I have a bunch of photos. In fact, if you want to have our photos of the fish we caught with worms right from this spot, I'll let you have those. You'll have a good campaign. So, we get so excited. And we take that, we get the sign ready for our next campaign, next Saturday's campaign. Now, incorporating a reason why. Tremendous difference. Now, we see a quantum leap in our persuasive power. We have a sign that now says, local worms catch more fish. And now, we notice that when the fishermen come down to the lake, they say, you know, kids, what's this all about? And our sales pitch becomes just what the research told us. That these fish love these local worms. That's what they eat normally, and so you can't get any better diet than that on your fish hook. That's, and you see the fish that work, we got these pictures from the old timers who used the worms that we're selling you, from the same place, to catch these big, beautiful fish. But notice the tremendous difference in selling power between, even if that had been a photo, worms that catch fish just to claim, but most, even direct marketing is at this level today. What is really needed, because so many people use that, next exit of escape, it's not believable.

I mean, if promises were a goal, all politicians would be fabulously wealthy, and we'd all love them. You know, we hold them in high disregard because promises are so cheap without proof. No. The price resistance will go right out the window when you meet all four of those, close off all four of those exits that prospects normally run out of. Problem, promise, proof, and proposition. So we've done that here. And notice that this is not brilliant copywriting. I mean, most ads are win or lose, not by the eloquence of the word, but just a psychology that you map out that matches up how your prospects are going to behave. Remember, the only power you have in the transaction is anticipating their next move, and it's always going to be for another objection unless you cover every objection, you know, the fourfold way. And something else very interesting happens that price resistance goes out the window. One great salesman that I worked with many years ago said, price is a funny thing. When people say that too expensive, what they're really saying to you is that you haven't given me enough benefit yet. And all the benefits that we've just mapped out in our persuasion equation with the worms, they're putting tremendous benefit and desire on one side of that apothecary scale. It means that you can throw a heavy price on the other side until they balance out. Just as I mentioned with the bass boat and everything, he is not going to, I mean, after all, he spent so much money, but he's going to bring home fish. And the cost is only about $2,000 a pound. So he's really getting a bargain for his all of this investment. So he's not going to worry about us charging $5 and $10. And maybe we don't even want to dig up worms anymore. Maybe we just want to sell the secret map of the lake and then branch out into an informational business. You can see that as the desire goes up, the price resistance goes down.

## [24:27 - 27:27]

I mean, you can equalize them. So the more you create desire and proof and a proposition with the promise that you're making, the higher price you can charge. In fact, you can even assign a value to each of these four key concepts, actually eight when you count all the words. Very simply, if when you ask to judge a piece of copy and add, and I think even in this room, if we were to hand out ads to each one of you, we'd get probably 100 different opinions on whether an ad is really good or not. I mean, the strawman case is the one that's really weak. We'd all probably recognize, but the other ones might give us a lot of different opinion. This brings all those opinions very close together. So if you're a copy chief or an art director or just an entrepreneur and wants to and you want to know, is this ad any good or not? Just ask, and you see how simple it is. Is it solving a problem? If it is for our audience or want to desire, give it 15 points. If it's solving or satisfying an urgent want or problem of desire, add another 10. And the same all the way through the list. Is it clearly making the promise? Is it doing it in a unique way? Give another 25 points. Is there strong proof? Give it 15 points. If it's really unquestionable, kind of proof that makes me say, wow, that's, I really have to, it's so believable, I have to see what this is all about and purchase it. Add another 10. And so on for proposition and use a friendly proposition, making it very desirable. And then if the audience is not satisfied, it's desirable. And then if the ad also used that, what we first said, opening the sale, the crackerjack secret, it's itself valuable so that we prevented the first objection. I have no time for this right now. I don't think it's too valuable to not give it time. Give it a 20-point bonus. So this adds up to 120 points. And the system that we created back when I was a copy chief, really focused on those, those, we didn't, at that time, realize how valuable it was to do editorial style layouts and mageologs. So this was the above the 20-point bonus that was, at that time, the how you can grade any ad. It's so simple. You don't have to, it's not rocket science. Is the ad solving a problem? Is it an urgent problem? And go through the whole list. And you'll very quickly see that 95% of the ads you'll see in any magazine. In fact, we have time tomorrow. You can do an experiment. Anybody rip out an ad, even a good, direct response, it will run through these points. And you don't have to need a slide rule or anything. Complicated, just ask these simple questions. Because these are the questions your prospects are asking as they encounter anything that you're presenting in front of them. So again, it doesn't teach you how to write. But at this point, I just want everybody on the same page. It's how do we evaluate? Because again, using that Zen Master philosophy, you have to see the target if you're going to hit the target. ## [27:27 - 27:34]
